mappings.rationale: Directly covers China from the Opium War through the 1911 Revolution, including Western challenges, the self-strengthening movement, and the Republican movement.
Directly covers the Meiji Restoration and Japan's modernization, including the abolition of feudalism, development of political parties, and emergence as a world power.
Western imperialism in China (opium trade, treaty ports, foreign concessions) and Japan's own emergence as an imperial power are central themes.
The development of Chinese reform and revolutionary movements and the cultivation of Japanese national loyalties during the Meiji era illustrate the rise of Asian nationalism.
The Taiping and Nien rebellions, peasant uprisings, and the 1911 Revolution in China represent major revolutionary movements covered in this section.
The collapse of the Chinese tributary system, Western demands for trade advantages and diplomatic parity, and the Sino-Japanese War involve diplomatic history.
The forced opening of China to Western trade, the opium trade, and Japan's rapid integration into the global economy illustrate early globalization processes.
Japan's development of zaibatsu cartels and industrial modernization, and China's struggles with Western commercial penetration, reflect the spread of capitalism in Asia.
Japan's rapid industrialization during the Meiji era and China's self-strengthening movement's focus on mining and weapons industry connect to the global spread of industrialization.
Western encroachments on China, the Sino-Japanese War, and Japan's emergence as a world power reshaped international relations in East Asia.
The fall of the Tokugawa, samurai opposition to the Meiji government, and the abolition of feudalism directly involved the samurai class.
Korea is explicitly discussed as a factor in the Sino-Japanese War of 1894-95 and in the broader context of East Asian geopolitics.
The contending forces of Chinese tradition versus westernization reflect the ongoing influence of Confucian values in late Qing society.
The constitutional movements in both China (after 1905) and Japan (creation of political parties, gradual development of representative institutions) involve the spread of democratic ideals.
Sun Yat-sen's United League and the 1911 Revolution provide the essential historical context for understanding Mao's later rise.
The loss of Central Asian territories, foreign encroachments, and the US Open Door policy reflect the geopolitical dimensions of late Qing China.
